entry.title: Step-By-Step Guide: Creating Your First RAP Application using OData V4 and Transporting to Quality
entry.content:
Introduction

In this blog, we will walk through the detailed steps to create your first RAP (RESTful ABAP Programming Model) application and correctly move it to the Quality system. This blog also highlights mandatory steps before transport and common mistakes that beginners usually make, based on real project experience.

Target Audience & Prerequisites

  • ABAP developers who are new to RAP
  • Basic knowledge of CDS Views and ABAP Development Tools (ADT)
  • System used: SAP S/4HANA (On-Premise / Private Cloud)
  • Exposure type: OData V4
  • Scenario covered: Basic Read-only RAP application

    Mandatory Objects in a RAP Application:

    • Package
    • Interface CDS View
    • Projection CDS View (Optional we can directly create service definition on Interface view also)
    • Service Definition
    • Service Binding
  1. Create Package: 

    Create a package in ADT:

    • Right-click on project → New → ABAP Package
    • Enter package name and description
    • Do not create it as a local package ($TMP)
    • Capture it in a transport request     
    • Only transportable packages can be moved to Quality and Production systems.

    •  

  2. Interface CDS View: A CDS View is a virtual data model in ABAP that defines how data from database tables is accessed and represented. In RAP, it often serves as the “Interface View.”

    1. Why It Matters:

      • Reusable logic: Centralizes data access for transactional and analytical purposes.
      • Foundation: Serves as the starting point for business object modeling in RAP.

    2. Steps:

      • Right-click your database table (e.g., ZTRAVEL_RAJ_M), select New Data Definition.
      • Provide a name, description, and use the Define View Entity template.
      • Assign to transport, activate, and check for errors.

      •  

  3. Service Definition: The Service Definition specifies which data elements (entities) of a RAP business object are exposed as OData services.

    1. Why It Matters:

      • Exposure Layer: Decides what consumers (e.g., Fiori apps, external APIs) can interact with.
      • It’s the contract between your backend logic and external consumers.
                                                                                                         

    2. Steps:

      • Right-click on the projection view, select New Service Definition.
      • Name, description, assign transport, and activate.
      • This exposes your RAP objects as a service.

      •  

  4. Service Binding: Service Binding links a service definition to an actual communication protocol (such as OData V4) and activation state.
    1. Why It Matters:
      • Enables Consumption: Without binding, your service cannot be called or used.
      • Protocol Choice: Determines how front ends communicate with your service (e.g., OData V4 for Fiori/UI5 apps).

    2. Steps:

      • Right-click on the service definition, select New Service Binding.
      • Choose OData V4 protocol (for most Fiori/RAP apps).
      • Assign transport, describe, finish, and activate.

      •  

      •  

  5. Why You Should NOT Publish from ADT: Publishing directly from ADT is meant only for local testing.

    1. Issues with ADT Publishing

      • Service is registered as a local ($TMP) object
      • Cannot be transported to Quality or Production
      • Not supported for productive landscapes

    2. Correct Way: Publish via /IWFND/V4_ADMIN

      • Transaction /IWFND/V4_ADMIN is the recommended way to publish RAP services.

    3. Steps: 
      • Open /IWFND/V4_ADMIN and click Publish Service Groups

      •  

      • Enter System Alias = LOCAL, Service Binding name click on get service groups

      •  

      • Select service and Publish

      •  

      • From the service group list, right click on your service group and click on transport publishing. It will prompt for customizing TR enter the transport and save. After that Click on transport routing assignment and capture in the customizing TR.

      •  

      •  

      • Service is now published

      •  

  6. Preview: Finally, let’s preview the application to verify that the service is working as expected. 

    •  

Conclusion: 

  • In this blog, we learned how to create a basic RAP application and the correct approach to publish and transport OData V4 services to the Quality system.
  • Using /IWFND/V4_ADMIN ensures transport safety and helps avoid common deployment issues that beginners often face.

Note: 

To customize the user interface of your RAP application, add appropriate UI annotations in the CDS view (interface or projection, depending on your design). Common annotations include @ui.headerInfo, @ui.lineItem, and @ui.selectionField.

After publishing the service, share the service URL (available in the Service Binding) with the Fiori development team so they can create the required Fiori tile.
After transporting to the Quality system, SICF activation is a manual step. Without activating the relevant SICF nodes, the service will not be accessible. Coordinate with your Basis team to complete this step.

entry.title: SAP UX Q1/2026 Update – Part 1: AI, Joule, SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Mobile Start
entry.content:
This series of posts gives an overview of user experience innovations in the cloud from the last six months, covering our whole portfolio, such as AI and Joule, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602, SAP SuccessFactors etc., as well as covering  innovations in our UI technologies for building great UX.

In this first post, we’ll look at the latest cross-suite UX innovations, coming from Joule, SAP Work Zone and SAP Mobile Start. Highlights are the general availability of Joule Agent Builder as well as Document AI for SAP Mobile Start, and the two brand new innovations in beta and early adopter care respectively: Joule Deep Research and Joule Action Bar, taking user experience to the next level.

Here are the links to the other posts in the series published so far:

  • Part 2: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602 and SAP Fiori Launchpad (generally available innovations).
  • Part 3: AI (Beta) Innovations in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602.
  • Part 4: SAP SuccessFactors (AI, Joule and More).
  • Part 5: Innovations in SAP Integrated Business Planning.

Before we get started: if you haven’t already seen them, do have a look at the overview of public cloud UX innovations I posted in Q3/2025 (parts 2 to 7 are linked in part 1):

  • SAP UX Q3/2025 Update – Part 1: AI, Joule, SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Mobile Start.

This upcoming series of posts includes Joule and AI UX highlights from individual products within the SAP Business Suite, but for a more comprehensive overview of our recent AI innovations, not just UX related, have a look at:

  • SAP Business AI: Release Highlights Q3 2025.
  • SAP Business AI: Release Highlights Q4/2025.
  • SAP Innovation Guide H2/2025 – SAP Business AI.

 

Joule User Experience Innovations

Joule is the AI-powered copilot for SAP systems, offering a powerful new user experience, using natural language to access our cloud products and providing a seamless flow for the user wherever they are, on desktop, laptop or mobile devices.

First, we’ll have a look at the latest generic or cross-product UX innovations, before I give you some pointers to the product-specific innovations as well as news about developing custom capabilities with the generally available Agent Builder.

This section covers these user experience innovations:

  • Joule Small Talk Pattern – beyond just small talk.
  • Use Joule now in 13 languages (2 more added).
  • Joule, Deep research capability (Beta).
  • Joule Action Bar (EAC).
  • Joule on Mobile Devices – now for SAP Sales Cloud too.
  • Joule Analytics Center For Analysing Usage.
  • Joule for Consultants: UX enhancement (Prompt Guide and Library).
  • Product-specific capabilities.
  • Customer-specific Agents: Agent Builder Available Now.

Joule Small Talk Pattern – Beyond just Small Talk

Initially, you could say hello to Joule, and Joule would reply in a friendly way – in other words, Joule could do small talk. In the mean time, we have significantly expanded the degree to which Joule supports general conversations.

Figure 1 shows an example, where the user has asked Joule the following:

I’m an HR manager preparing a script for a new hire onboarding presentation. The focus is on our company’s commitment to employee development, mentorship, and overall well-being. Please help me draft clear and engaging talking points that highlight why mentorship and professional growth are core values in our organization. Use the mission statement “help the world to run better.

To which Joule responds with a structured answer, with sections containing bullet points with suggestions.


Figure 1: An example of Joule small talk pattern, going beyond just small talk, shown with Joule in full-screen mode. Alt Text: Screenshot of Joule with the example query shown at the top, and below that Joule’s answer with sections “Introduction”, “Commitment to Employee Development” and “Mentorship as a Core Value” visible.

Figure 2 gives an overview of all the five interaction patterns supported by Joule today – the four patterns which interact with an SAP system, plus the small talk pattern.


Figure 2: The five Joule interaction patterns. Alt Text: Five images of Joule showing from left to right the Informational pattern (“What?”), Navigational (“Where?”), Transactional (“Do”), Analytical (“Show me”) and Small Talk (“Be creative”).

Use Joule in 13 Languages – Two More Added

Since my last update, we have added support for Arabic and Indonesian, bringing the total up to 13. Here they are in alphabetical order, with the variant of the language in brackets.

  • Arabic
  • Chinese (Simplified)
  • English (US)
  • French (France)
  • German (Germany)
  • Greek
  • Indonesian
  • Japanese
  • Korean
  • Polish
  • Portuguese (Brazil)
  • Spanish (Spain)
  • Vietnamese

In addition to these, we have four more languages available as a preview that are not yet officially supported:

  • Czech
  • Dutch (Netherlands)
  • Italian
  • Turkish

To get the most up-to-date information about language support for Joule, look at the documentation:

  • Joule Multi Language Support.

Joule, Deep Research Capability (Beta)

Currently available for beta-testing, the deep research capability means that Joule can provide much deeper insights in response to queries, by using external web resources in addition to those from your organization and SAP data.

One of our AI UX principles is “human in control”, and hence we let users choose when they want a deep research analysis instead of the standard analysis (Figure X). Similarly, the deep research is done in two stages, where first the system shows the user the steps it plans to take in order to do the research, and asks the user to confirm before continuing (as shown in Figure Y).

Figures 3 – 7 give you an idea of what it currently looks like, starting with the user selecting the deep research option, as shown in Figure 2. Joule then displays the research approval request, summarizing the steps it plans to take (Figure 4). Once the user has approved this, the research is done and the outcome shown. Finally, the user can expand the Sources section to get links to articles on which the research was based. In this case, the sources were all external.


Figure 3: Users can choose the answer type “Deep Research” (currently Beta) and then enter their question. Alt Text: Two images showing the text entry field for Joule, the upper one showing the context menu with the options for Answer Types “Default” and “Deep Research”, the lower one showing a “Deep Research” token displayed in the field, with a query entered “What is the name and weight of the eBike MZ-FG-E164, compare it to the average weight of similar bikes found online for the top 3 market sellers in only Germany region in the past 6 months”.


Figure 4: Joule deep research (beta) provides research approval request to the user. Alt Text: Image showing the Joule providing a formatted text with “Research Approval Request” in large bold font as the title, with sections “Research Plan” and “Scope”, each with bullet points outlining what Joule plans to do, such as “Look up the eBike model in internal systems…”, “Research online sources..”.


Figure 5: The outcome of the deep research – first page. Alt Text: Image showing that the user prompted “Start Deep Research”, followed by the beginning of the outcome text: the title in bold (“Comparison of … to Top German eBike Sellers’ Models”), with sections “Overview” and “Key Findings”.


Figure 6: The outcome of the deep research – second page. Alt Text: Image showing the section “Analysis” with sub-sections “Weight Comparison by Category” and “Comparative Analysis”, followed by the beginning of the section “Conclusion”.


Figure 7: The outcome of the deep research, with links to sources. Alt Text: Image showing the remainder of the Conclusion, with a sub-section “Data Access Notice”, followed by a list of five sources with their titles and links to each of them.

To get an even better idea of how it works, watch it in action, or read more:

  • Video: Joule Deep Research example (2:06 minutes)
  • SAP Discovery Center: Joule, Deep research capability

Joule Action Bar (EAC)

The Joule Action Bar is an intelligent overlay in the browser that delivers AI on any application – SAP and beyond.

It is available to early adopters as part of our early adopter care (EAC) program, allowing us to gather feedback from participating customers and iterate further before releasing it to everyone.

The Joule Action Bar hovers over your browser content and understands what users are currently doing. Powered by WalkMe’s unique technology that interprets screen-based content, it can proactively step in to provide guidance, as well as being called up by the user on-demand. A particularly powerful feature is personalization: users can personalize the experience by creating custom AI actions for everyday tasks, not only for SAP systems, also for others.

Figures 8 and 9 show an example where the action bar is being used to deal with a support ticket in ServiceNow that indicates that urgent action is required. Normally, the user would have to switch to a separate SAP screen and analyze the issue, but the Joule Action Bar provides a summary of the pertinant information such at your fingertips – in this case the impact summary.


Figure 8: Joule action bar visible while processing a ticket in ServiceNow. Alt Text: A screenshot with the Joule action bar visible at the bottom of the screen, on top of a greyed-out ServiceNow ticket screen. The action bar offers icons to open Joule, and buttons “Suggestions”, “View supply chaing impact”, “Tools” and personalization.


Figure 9: Joule action bar providing a summary of the supply chain impact. Alt Text: Image showing a pop-over “View supply chain impact” above the Joule action bar, summarizing the impact, explaining stock availability and providing a risk alert.

It’s main benefits are:

  • Always available in a user’s flow of work.
  • Detecting user needs in real time and offering help without being asked.
  • Proactively recommends next best actions based on screen-based and business context.
  • One consistent, unified and intuitive AI experience across SAP and non-SAP applications.
  • Personalization: allows users to create custom AI actions for their typical activities.

To get an even better idea of how it works, watch this short video:

  • Video: Joule Action Bar (1:15 minutes)
  • SAP Discovery Center: WalkMe, SAP Joule action bar (Proactive).
  • SAP Discovery Center: WalkMe, SAP Joule action bar (On-Demand).

Joule on Mobile Devices – now for SAP Sales Cloud too

Joule has been available via SAP Mobile Start since Q1/2025 (as announced in this blog post), and is now also available directly from within the SAP Sales Cloud mobile app, supporting these three patterns:

  • Transactional: Joule can handle tasks like updating status, expected value, sales phase of opportunities, and more, directly within the chat interface.
  • Navigational: Joule can guide users to specific pages within the app based on their requests, such as directing them to where they can open a sales deal.
  • Informational: Joule can access documentation and knowledge bases to answer questions about company policies, and other relevant information, providing summaries and links to related resources.


Figure 10: Joule now available in the SAP Sales Cloud mobile app. Alt Text: Four images of iPhones showing (1) the Joule start page with suggestions for prompts such as “My Opportunities”, (2) a card showing three opportunities, (3) the user prompted “Tell me about 20653”, Joule shows a card with details about the Sales Deal and suggests further prompts such as “Update status”, (4) the user prompted “My Accounts”, Joule shows a card with one account and suggests further prompts such as “Show Current Challenges”.

Joule Analytics Center For Analysing Usage

Knowing what users are using and how they are using it, as well as what they are not using, is a key ingredient of user research and key to constantly improving their user experience over time.

The Joule Analytics Center for Joule is now generally available, allowing customers to get precisely these kind of insights for Joule. It provide an interactive dashboard, where users can filter on:

  • Interaction type (Direct response, Scenario, Information retrieval, Slot filling, File upload)
  • Product name
  • Scenario name
  • Client type (desktop, mobile, Microsoft Copilot)
  • Date range.

The dashboard includes a summary and charts. To find out more, check out the documentation:

  • Joule Analytics Center.

Product-specific capabilities

In addition to the above generic capabilities, our products provide a large number of Joule skills for getting your work done more efficiently, as well as a handful of the new agents discussed above. For a comprehensive overview of Joule capabilities, including product-specific skills available today, here’s where to go:

  • Joule Capabilities (online documentation, starting with What’s New and then listing capabilities for each SAP product)

Customer-specific Agents: Agent Builder Available Now

Joule Agents significantly enhance the overall user experience, as opposed to the user interface, because the system will do a lot more for you based on your requests – especially if you have multi-agents working on a complex workflow. As a result, the overall experience you have using SAP goes up a level, since you can get a lot done much more easily than before.

I’m pleased to say that the Agent builder in Joule Studio is now generally available. You can access it easily from SAP Build, as shown in Figure 3.


Figure 11: Create an AI Agent or a Joule Skill with SAP Build. Alt Text: Screenshot from SAP Build with the “Create Project” pop-over giving the user four illustrated cards to choose “What would you like to build?”: “Application”, “Automated Process”, “AI Agent and Joule Skill (highlighted)”, “Business Site”.

For more information about building your own agents, have a look at this blog post by Michael Ameling,  watch the demo video and get details via the SAP Discovery Center:

  • Agent builder in Joule Studio is now generally available: Build your own Joule agents today!
  • Video: How to Build, Test and Deploy AI Agents with Agent Builder in Joule Studio (2:11 minutes)
  • SAP Discovery Center: Joule studio, agent builder.

Joule for Consultants: UX Enhancement (Prompt Guide and Library)

You can now ask Joule for Consultants to show Quick Links, resulting in an overview of available links: to the new prompt guide and prompt library, as well as links to what’s new and to open the console, as shown in Figure 12.


Figure 12: Quick Links in SAP Joule for Consultants. Alt Text: Image showing Joule in full screen mode, with the user prompt “Quick Links” at the top, followed by two cards side-by-side with illustrations: the left one showing hands typing on a keyboard, above a text explaining the prompt library and two links “Show Prompt Guide” and “Open Prompt Library”. The right card shows an image of a console screen, a description of the console and two links “Show What’s New” and “Open Console”.

The prompt guide gives advice on how to formulate effective prompts, including examples of well-formulated ones and less effective ones, as you can see in Figure 13.


Figure 13: The Prompt Guide in SAP Joule for Consultants. Alt Text: Screenshot of Joule in full-screen mode, with the chat area showing a card on the left with a list of recommendations, the first one “Define Your Objective Clearly Basic Prompt” is highlighted. On the right a card with Prompt Details, with sections “Effective Example”, “Less Effective Style”, “Description”, “Type of Prompt”, and “Role”.

The prompt library lets you browse or search for pre-defined prompts relevant for common roles (such as Technology Consultant, Project Manager) or areas (such as UI5/Fiori Development, Security Best Practices, System Architecture Design). Each prompt comes with a brief description of its objective, an example of a similar prompt with a less effective style, and a longer description. The description explains why the suggested prompt is effective, gives hints on how to extend it to your needs, and also explains the shortcomings of the less effective example. Figure 14 shows the prompt library, with details for the selected prompt.


Figure 14: The Prompt Library in SAP Joule for Consultants. Alt Text: Screenshot of Joule for Consultant Console, with the two areas with a vertical separator that users can move left or right. On the left three columns of cards showing pre-defined prompts are shown, with “S/4HANA Legacy Integration Strategy” selected. On the right details of the prompt in sections “Prompt”, “Less Effective Style”, “Objective”, and “Description”.

 Find out more in this blog post:

  • SAP Joule for Consultants: Listening, Learning, and Delivering.

To learn more about Joule for Consultants overall, watch this video or take the new learning journey:

  • Video: Joule for Consultants demo (12:02 minutes).
  • Learning Journey: Accelerating SAP Project Delivery Using SAP Joule for Consultants

Further information on Joule

If you want an overview of what’s new, or want to set up Joule in your landscape, have a look at these regular blog posts from Shanna Bauer, or look at the documentation:

  • From Hype to Hands-On: Level Up Your AI Skills This January (contains links to the posts from October 2025 onwards at the end).
  • Joule documentation (including What’s New, integration guide and more).
  • Joule overview (PDF file).

For an overview of what is planned, have a look at the road map for Joule in the SAP Road Map Explorer:

  • Joule Road Map.

 

SAP Build Work Zone

SAP Build Work Zone users and administrators benefit from these recent innovations – note that when I refer to “Advanced Edition”, that also covers SAP SuccessFactors Work Zone:

  • Advanced Edition: Now with Joule.
  • Advanced Edition: New menu experience.
  • Advanced Edition: Spaces and pages available.
  • Standard Edition: New shell bar, custom logos easier.

In addition to giving users concrete benefits in usability, these updates also contribute to our goal of providing a consistent user experience across the entire SAP Business Suite. They implement some of the design updates that I announced last year in my blog post Upcoming Design Updates for the SAP Business Suite (SAP Fiori for Web), with the goal to further increase consistency, helping users to feel confident and at ease when using a suite of products – no unexpected surprises.

Advanced Edition: Now with Joule

Having already been available in the standard edition, as well as SAP Start and SAP Mobile Start, Joule is now finally also available in the SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition, as shown in Figure 15. You can find out more in this blog post, including information on how to set it up:

  • Joule is Now Integrated into SAP Build Work Zone - Advanced Edition


Figure 15: Joule in SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition. Alt Text: Screenshot showing the Joule start page bottom right over the advanced edition home page.

Advanced Edition: New Menu Experience

The new menu experience that is being introduced across the SAP Business Suite is now available for SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition. It supports all three options specified by the SAP Design System, as you can see in Figure 16. Offering this ensures a consistent experience for accessing menus across all products in the suite.


Figure 16: New menu experience for SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition. From left to right: collapse mode, full width mode, and drop-down mode. Alt Text: Three screenshot snippets showing a menu with entries (from top to bottom): Home, My Workspace, Recently Visitied Workspaces, Favorites, View All Workspaces, New Workspace, Applications, Knowledge Base. Each has its own icon; in collapse mode, only the icons are visible, for the others the text is visible too.

Owners of sites also benefit from the enhanced menu editor, shown in Figure 17. They can choose whether to use the default mode or whether to use the overlay mode. Also (not shown), they can configure which icon to use for each entry in the menu.


Figure 17: New menu editor for SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition. Alt Text: Screenshot of the menu editor showing the menu items and options on the left, with a preview on the right.

Advanced Edition: Spaces and Pages Available

Business users now benefit from the Spaces and Pages design, as pioneered by the SAP Fiori launchpad, and available for the standard edition since 2023. Figure 18 shows an example of what this looks like.


Figure 18: Spaces and Pages in SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition. Alt Text: Screenshot of the home page of the advanced edition with spaces and pages: four spaces are listed in the top row, from left to right “Employee Self Services” (selected), “Finance”, “News”, and “Projects”. Below that the page shows five tiles and a large card “My Goals” with a horizontal bar chart showing goal completion percentages for the 3 goals.

Content administrators of SAP Build Work Zone can activate Spaces and Pages - New Experience view mode in the site settings, then proceed to create spaces and pages manually, and add UI integration cards or tiles to each page. The new site experience displays spaces and pages that are federated from remote content providers side by side to spaces and pages that are created locally, as well as displaying pages that originate from a content package.

With this, SAP Mobile Start can now visualize spaces and pages, including UI integration cards, from all SAP Build Work Zone editions.

Find out more about the new site experience in the documentation, and about SAP Mobile Start support:

  • Documentation: New Experience View Mode
  • Blog post: Support of SAP Build Work Zone, advanced and much more: SAP Mobile Start V2.2 & 2.3 - Release Update

Standard Edition: New Shell Bar, Custom Logos Easier

New Shell Bar

We have standardized the shell bar for the SAP Business Suite, and now SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition has introduced a new compliant shell bar. For users the main difference is that the product name is now displayed next to the SAP Logo, as you can see on the left in Figure 19.

Some custom plugins or test automation which accesses elements in the shell bar, such as the user menu, may need to be adapted, because the old shell bar has been replaced by a web component implementation. For this reason, we currently only have the new shell bar switched on by default for new sites. For existing sites, we ask customers to test their plugins and automations in test sites first and then to enable the new shell. We currently plan to phase out the old shell header as follows, and as described in detail in KBA 3673380:

  1. Beginning Q2/2026 we plan to activate the new shell header for all sites, but customers have the option to opt out by switching it off temporarily.
  2. In Q3/2026, the new shell header will be the only option – no opt-out any more.

Custom Logos Easier to Add

Customers can now replace the SAP log with a custom logo directly, without having to create a whole new visual theme. Often, customers only want to customize this one aspect, which now becomes much easier to do. Figure 19 shows an example, where the SAP logo (shown on the left) has been replaced by a Work Zone logo (shown on the right).


Figure 19: Replacing the SAP logo (shown on the left) with a custom logo (on the right). Alt Text: Screenshot snippet showing the top left of the page, with the logos visible top left.

 

SAP Mobile Start

The SAP Mobile Start 2.4 release brings a number of innovations. The one I would like to highlight here is SAP Document AI integration (shown below).

SAP Mobile Start 2.5 brings these innovations:

  • Support for Apple’s new Liquid Glass design language (shown below)
  • Additional filter option for To-Dos, allowing filtering by semantic groups defined in SAP Task Center.
  • A small but important detail: empty pages are not shown in the App screen anymore.

You can find links to more information about these two releases at the end of the section.

SAP Document AI Integration

With SAP Document AI now integrated, users can scan or upload documents directly from SAP Mobile Start, which are then processed by the SAP Document AI service. For example, if you need to scan and upload a quality certificate, you can start the upload process right from SAP Mobile Start, and the SAP Document AI service takes care of all necessary follow-on steps, such as extracting data and assigning them to the right business objects.  Figure 20 shows an example.


Figure 20: Document AI integrated in SAP Mobile Start. Alt Text: Three iPhone images: (1) the start page, with the card “SAP Document AI” listed as the first in “Favorites”; (2) a list of three possible document types, with “Standard Delivery Note” selected; (3) the camera showing an image of a delivery note, and a “Save (1)” button.

Apple’s Liquid Glass design language supported

With Liquid Glass, Apple has unified its platform design language across Apple’s ecosystem of products. Liquid Glass provides a much more dynamic and expressive user experience and complements the experience of latest Apple devices like the Apple iPhone 17.

The update gives users some concrete benefits beyond the “look”: you now get more space on the screen since the navigation bar collapses if it is not needed. Furthermore, the content scrolls below the navigation and header bar.


Figure 21: SAP Mobile Start on iPhone with the new Liquid Glass design. Alt Text: Two iPhone images showing how some of the content shines through icons that are in the foreground, such as the “Start” icon at the bottom over a “Customer Factsheet” card.

To find out more about the above innovations as well as what is planned in our road map, have a look at:

  • SAP Mobile Start V2.4: SAP Document AI integration and much more. Release Update.
  • SAP Mobile Start V2.5 - Release update brings UX and To-Do enhancements.
  • SAP Mobile Start Road Map.

 

Continue Reading

I hope you enjoyed this first part, covering cross-product UX services. Here's a link to part 2, covering a large number of UX innovations that are generally available with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602 and SAP Fiori launchpad:

  • SAP UX Q1/2026 Update – Part 2: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602 and SAP Fiori Launchpad.

Do keep posting your experiences and recommendations yourself in the SAP Community, with the SAP Fiori and/or the User Experience tag! In case you are wondering how to get a list of the most recent blog posts on SAP Fiori and User Experience in our new SAP Community, use these links:

  • Latest blog posts on SAP Fiori in the SAP Community.
  • Latest blog posts on User Experience in the SAP Community.

entry.title: My Journey Building a Mobile‑Friendly Fiori App Using AI and Vibe Coding
entry.content:
and saga continues...

Figure 1. Screenshots from mobile phone that show main app functionality

 

UPDATE from my previous posts:

https://blogs.sap.com/2016/08/16/build-your-own-sap-fiori-app-in-the-cloud-2016-edition/

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-members/kiko3-sap-fiori-user-administration/

Please read my previous blog so you can familiarize yourself and be up to speed with the current blog post.


My idea was to enable basic user‑administration tasks — locking and unlocking users, setting a new initial password, and adjusting a user’s validity date — to be performed directly from a mobile phone instead of requiring a laptop. This isn’t meant for everyday office use, but it’s extremely helpful in global companies with on‑call teams supporting users in multiple time zones.

A typical scenario for this app is when you’re away from your desk — for example, sitting in a park or waiting at an airport — and you suddenly need to perform one of these tasks. Instead of opening your laptop and spending 15–30 minutes connecting and navigating through the system, you can complete everything on your mobile device in under five minutes.

Your phone can first connect to the corporate network through a VPN app, and the 2FA code arrives on the same device. From there, you simply open the corporate URL linked to the Fiori app. After setting the new initial password and unlocking the user, you can immediately send an email notification containing the credentials you just generated.

This approach makes user‑administration tasks faster, more convenient, and perfectly suited for real‑world on‑call situations.

Let’s look at how a typical real‑world scenario usually unfolds — and whether there’s room to improve the process. In most cases, it consists of just 2–3 simple steps.

Step 1

Ivana (SAP user): I’m locked out of the SAP system. Could you please unlock my user?
You: Your user has been unlocked.

…a few minutes later…

Step 2

Ivana (SAP user): Could you please reset my password and unlock my user again? It seems I forgot it.
You: Your user is unlocked and the new initial password is: XXXXXX.

…a few minutes later…

Step 3

Ivana (SAP user): My SAP user is no longer valid…
You: Please try now — I’ve extended your validity date for two weeks.

One thing that first came to my mind is when you unlocking user, adding additional check if validity date is in the past, also if you are setting new initial password for user that is already locked unlock it and also check if validity date is in the past. If you find that validity date is in the past show warning message about validity date, as showed in Figure 2.

 Figure 2. Screenshots shows setting new initial password, automatically unlocking and warning message for validity date in past 

When I started building this app, I had no idea there was a name for what I was doing. I was simply copying pieces of code from my custom Fiori app, pasting them into an AI chat window, and asking it to “add this” or “fix that.” It felt a bit like having a super‑fast coding partner who never sleeps and never complains.

Every time I uploaded a new version, I hoped it would work — and sometimes it did. Out of 110 versions I tried, around 30 were perfectly fine. The rest were a mix of broken logic, misunderstandings, or classic AI hallucinations. In other words, about 27% of the time, things worked exactly the way I imagined.

But here’s the interesting part: even with all the trial and error, I managed to implement every single idea that came to mind. I would think of a feature, describe it to the AI, and a few minutes later I had a working version (or at least something close enough to refine). Only later did I discover that this whole approach actually has a name — vibe coding — and that many developers are already using it to speed up their workflow.

Looking back, the process wasn’t perfect, but it was surprisingly effective. And honestly, it was fun — like building something together with an unpredictable but incredibly creative teammate.

I have always using new increment in version when uploading  (Name of SAPUI5 App) custom Fiori to avoid issue with cache.

SE38 -> /UI5/UI5_REPOSITORY_LOAD

 Figure 3. captures the different versions I uploaded as I worked through this entire process

Version History
Added SAP theme selection option
Added application logo
Implemented Lock/Unlock functionality with automatic refresh after each operation
Added unlock warning when the user’s validity date is in the past
Improved password reset flow: when setting a new initial password, the app automatically unlocks the user (if locked) and checks the validity date
Enhanced validity date handling: when updating validity, the app now suggests a default date 10 days from today and refreshes after saving
Added quick‑filter buttons:
 - Show Locked (users with uflag > 0)
 - Show Invalid (users with a validity date in the past)
Added SID/Client input field before the Search bar
Improved Search behavior: entering two or more characters automatically displays matching users
Added in ABAP code option to search by last name/surname

For demo and full functionality of this Fiori app, please check video on Youtube.

Conclusion

What began as an experiment turned into a practical tool that saves time, reduces friction, and makes on‑call support far more manageable. With AI‑assisted development becoming more accessible, this is just the beginning. I’m excited to see how these new workflows will continue to reshape the way we build and interact with enterprise applications.

NOTE: All screenshots and the video were taken on an iPhone 17, using the Demo21 S/4HANA 2022 – 01 (02/2023) FPS environment.

Feel free to share your thoughts on this article or suggest any features you’d like to see added to the app. 😃

entry.title: SAP UI5 - Component-Based Architecture: Shell, Feature and Modules
entry.content:
• 1. Shell
  • App View
  • App Controller
  • Shell View
  • Shell Controller
  • Shell Manifest
  • Shell Component
  • 2. Feature List
  • App View
  • App Controller
  • List View
  • List Controller
  • List Manifest
  • 3. Feature Detail
  • Detail View
  • Detail Controller
  • Detail Manifest
  • 4. Modules
  • 5. Communication between Components
  • Event handling with sap.ui.core.ComponentRegistry
  • Passing an API
  • 6. Conclusion

This post describes one possible way to structure larger Freestyle SAP UI5 applications in a meaningful way.

The focus here is on a component-based architecture, where the application is split into a parent shell and several functional feature components, following a structure that worked well in our projects.

In this compact example, the application is hierarchically split into multiple UI5 components.

Each component is technically a standalone UI5 application with its own Component.js and its own manifest.
This way, the application grows in a controlled manner and can be extended at any time with new features and modules.

We use a classic list/detail application here. Shell as the framework, Feature List as the entry point, and Feature Detail for the object view.
Modules are smaller (reusable) building blocks within a feature.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

1. Shell

The Shell is a standalone UI5 application and represents the technical framework of the application.

It renders the feature components at the appropriate places and is otherwise responsible for layout, navigation, or UI states (e.g. busy states).

App View

The App view is the root view of the Shell and is only responsible for the pure UI structure.

The Shell view is loaded into the FlexibleColumnLayout.

<mvc:View
  controllerName="app.shell.controller.App"
  displayBlock="true"
  height="100%"
  width="100%"
  xmlns="sap.m"
  xmlns:f="sap.f"
  xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc">
  <App 
    id="app"
    busy="{appView>/busy}">
      <f:FlexibleColumnLayout
        id="layout"
        layout="{appView>/layout}"
        backgroundDesign="Translucent" />
  </App>
</mvc:View>

App Controller

The corresponding controller does not contain any notable logic except for initializing the view model appView.

sap.ui.define([
  "app/utility/controller/BaseController",
  "sap/ui/model/json/JSONModel"
], function (
  BaseController,
  JSONModel
) {
  "use strict";

  return BaseController.extend("app.shell.controller.App", {

    onInit: function () {
      const oViewModel = this._createViewModel();
      this.setModel(oViewModel, "appView");

      this.getView().addStyleClass(this.getOwnerComponent().getContentDensityClass());
    },

    _createViewModel: function () {
      return new JSONModel({
        busy: false,
        delay: 0,
        layout: "OneColumn",
        previousLayout: ""
      });
    }
  });
});

Shell View

We keep the Shell view fairly simple.

It contains a layout and an area (the mainContents aggregation) where feature components are rendered, e.g. using a ToolPage.

<mvc:View
  controllerName="app.shell.controller.Shell"
  xmlns="sap.m"
  xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
  xmlns:tnt="sap.tnt"
  xmlns:core="sap.ui.core"
  displayBlock="true">
  <tnt:ToolPage id="toolPage" sideExpanded="false">
    <tnt:sideContent>
      <tnt:SideNavigation id="sideNavigation">
        <tnt:item>
          <tnt:NavigationList id="navList" expanded="false" selectedKey="">
            <tnt:NavigationListItem
              id="navListItemList"
              text="List"
              icon="sap-icon://list"
              select="onNavToList" />
          </tnt:NavigationList>
        </tnt:item>
      </tnt:SideNavigation>
    </tnt:sideContent>
    <tnt:mainContents>
    </tnt:mainContents>
  </tnt:ToolPage>
</mvc:View>

Shell Controller

After the Shell has been loaded, we directly navigate to Feature List and render it into mainContents of the ToolPage.

In addition, we define two event listeners for the events "setShellBusy" and "hideShellBusy" for later communication.

sap.ui.define([
  "app/utility/controller/BaseController",
  "sap/ui/model/json/JSONModel",
  "sap/ui/Device"
], function (
  BaseController,
  JSONModel,
  Device
) {
  "use strict";

  return BaseController.extend("app.shell.controller.Shell", {

    onInit: function () {
      const oViewModel = this._createViewModel();
      this.setModel(oViewModel, "shellView");
      this.getRouter().getRoute("shell").attachPatternMatched(this._onShellPatternMatched, this);
      this.getOwnerComponent().attachSetShellBusy(this._setShellBusy, this);
      this.getOwnerComponent().attachHideShellBusy(this._hideShellBusy, this);
    },

    _onShellPatternMatched: function () {
      this._navToList();
    },

    _createViewModel: function () {
      return new JSONModel({
        busy: false,
        delay: 50
      });
    },

    _setShellBusy: function() {
      this.getModel("appView").setProperty("/busy", true);
    },
    
    _hideShellBusy: function() {
      this.getModel("appView").setProperty("/busy", false);
    },

    _navToList: function () {
      const bReplace = !Device.system.phone;
      this.getRouter().navTo("list", {}, bReplace);
    }
  });
});

Shell Manifest

The Shell knows its features via "componentUsages" in the manifest.

In our example, we want to display the List feature as the first feature within the Shell.

For this, we define the List feature once as a component in "componentUsages" and create a route.
The "controlAggregation" of List is the "mainContents" aggregation of the ToolPage in the view.

{
  "_version": "1.32.0",
  "sap.app": {
    "id": "app.shell",
    "type": "application",
    "i18n": "i18n/i18n.properties",
    "applicationVersion": { "version": "0.0.1" },
    "title": "{{appTitle}}",
    "description": "{{appDescription}}"
  },
  "sap.ui": {
    "technology": "UI5",
    "fullWidth": true,
    "deviceTypes": { "desktop": true, "tablet": true, "phone": true }
  },
  "sap.ui5": {
    "dependencies": {
      "minUI5Version": "1.120.0",
      "libs": {
        "sap.ui.core": {},
        "sap.m": {},
        "sap.f": {},
        "sap.tnt": {}
      }
    },
    "models": {
      "i18n": {
        "type": "sap.ui.model.resource.ResourceModel",
        "settings": { "bundleName": "app.shell.i18n.i18n" }
      }
    },
    "componentUsages": {
      "list": {
        "name": "app.list",
        "lazy": false
      }
    },
    "routing": {
      "config": {
        "routerClass": "sap.f.routing.Router",
        "viewType": "XML",
        "viewPath": "app.shell.view",
        "controlId": "layout",
        "controlAggregation": "beginColumnPages",
        "async": true,
        "bypassed": {
          "target": "shell"
        }
      },
      "routes": [
        {
          "pattern": "",
          "name": "shell",
          "target": "shell"
        },
        {
          "pattern": "List",
          "name": "list",
          "target": ["shell", "list"]
        }
      ],
      "targets": {
        "shell": {
          "viewName": "Shell",
          "viewId": "shell",
          "viewLevel": 1
        },
        "list": {
          "type": "Component",
          "usage": "list",
          "parent": "shell",
          "controlId": "toolPage",
          "controlAggregation": "mainContents"
        }
      }
    },
    "rootView": {
      "viewName": "app.shell.view.App",
      "type": "XML",
      "async": true,
      "id": "app"
    }
  }
}

Shell Component

Besides the usual router initialization, we only provide the two events already mentioned: setShellBusy and hideShellBusy.

sap.ui.define([
	"app/shell/model/models",
	"sap/ui/core/UIComponent"
], function(
	models,
	UIComponent
) {
	"use strict";

	return UIComponent.extend("app.shell.Component", {
		metadata: {
			manifest: "json",
			events: {		
				setShellBusy: {},
				hideShellBusy: {}
			}
		},
		init: function () {
            UIComponent.prototype.init.apply(this, arguments);
            this.setModel(models.createDeviceModel(), "device");
            this.getRouter().initialize();
        },
		destroy: function() {			
			UIComponent.prototype.destroy.apply(this, arguments);		
		},
		getContentDensityClass: function() {
			if (this._sContentDensityClass === undefined) {				
				if (document.body.classList.contains("sapUiSizeCozy") ||                
					document.body.classList.contains("sapUiSizeCompact")) {
					this._sContentDensityClass = "";				
				} else if (!Device.support.touch) {				
					this._sContentDensityClass = "sapUiSizeCompact";				
				} else {				
					this._sContentDensityClass = "sapUiSizeCozy";
				}
			}
			return this._sContentDensityClass;
		}
	});
});

2. Feature List

Feature components are also technically standalone UI5 applications and can be executed separately.

Similar to the Shell, we can link feature components (List/Detail) and also integrate smaller modules.

Feature List decides when to navigate to Detail.

In the case of a list/detail linking, we pass the necessary parameters during navigation. Apart from that, Feature List does not know any further details about Feature Detail.

App View

Structured similarly to the Shell. The FlexibleColumnLayout is important here for the interaction between List and Detail.

Layout handling is bound to the view model.

<mvc:View
  controllerName="app.list.controller.App"
  displayBlock="true"
  height="100%"
  xmlns="sap.m"
  xmlns:f="sap.f"
  xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc">
  <App id="app" height="100%">
    <f:FlexibleColumnLayout
      id="layout"
      height="100%"
      layout="{appView>/layout}"
      backgroundDesign="Translucent" />
  </App>
</mvc:View>

App Controller

Here we initialize the view model and add a new event listener for the component event setLayout.

sap.ui.define([
	"app/utility/controller/BaseController",
	"sap/ui/model/json/JSONModel"
], function (BaseController, JSONModel) {
  "use strict";

  return BaseController.extend("app.list.controller.App", {
    onInit: function () {
		const oViewModel = this._createViewModel();
		this.setModel(oViewModel, "appView");
		this.getOwnerComponent().attachSetLayout(this._onSetLayout, this);
    },

    _onSetLayout: function (oEvent) {
		const sLayout = oEvent.getParameter("layout");
		this.getModel("appView").setProperty("/layout", sLayout);
    },

    _createViewModel: function() {
		const oViewModel = new JSONModel({
				busy: false,
				delay: 0
			});
			return oViewModel;
		}
  });
});

List View

For simplicity, a normal list with some dummy items:

<mvc:View
  controllerName="app.list.controller.List"
  xmlns="sap.m"
  xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
  displayBlock="true">
  <Page title="List Feature" class="sapUiContentPadding">
    <content>
      <List id="demoList" mode="SingleSelectMaster" itemPress="onItemPress">
        <StandardListItem title="Item 1000" description="1000000000" type="Active" />
        <StandardListItem title="Item 2000" description="1000000001" type="Active" />
        <StandardListItem title="Item 3000" description="1000000002" type="Active" />
      </List>
    </content>
  </Page>
</mvc:View>

List Controller

Without any major binding logic, we have direct navigation to the Detail feature here, passing the item ID as a parameter.

sap.ui.define([
  "app/utility/controller/BaseController",
  "sap/ui/Device",
  "sap/ui/model/json/JSONModel"
], function (BaseController, Device, JSONModel) {
  "use strict";

  return BaseController.extend("app.list.controller.List", {

    onInit: function () {
      const oViewModel = this._createViewModel();
      this.setModel(oViewModel, "listView");
      this.getRouter().getRoute("list").attachPatternMatched(this._onListMatched, this);
    },

    _onListMatched: function () {
      // maybe further binding logic
    },

    onItemPress: function (oEvent) {
      const oItem = oEvent.getParameter("listItem");
      const sId = oItem.getDescription();

      const bReplace = !Device.system.phone;
      this.getRouter().navTo("detail", { id: sId }, bReplace);
    },

    _createViewModel: function () {
      const oViewModel = new JSONModel({
        busy: false,
        delay: 0,
        noDataText: this.getResourceBundle().getText("noDataText")
      });
      return oViewModel;
    }

  });
});

List Manifest

As in the Shell, we integrate the next feature (Detail) in the manifest of the Feature List component.

Feature Detail also receives its own route.

{
  "_version": "1.32.0",
  "sap.app": {
    "id": "app.list",
    "type": "component",
    "i18n": "i18n/i18n.properties",
    "applicationVersion": { "version": "0.0.1" },
    "title": "{{appTitle}}",
    "description": "{{appDescription}}"
  },
  "sap.ui": {
    "technology": "UI5",
    "fullWidth": true,
    "deviceTypes": { "desktop": true, "tablet": true, "phone": true }
  },
  "sap.ui5": {
    "dependencies": {
      "minUI5Version": "1.120.0",
      "libs": {
        "sap.ui.core": {},
        "sap.m": {},
        "sap.f": {}
      }
    },
    "componentUsages": {
      "detail": {
        "name": "app.detail",
        "lazy": false
      }
    },
    "contentDensities": { "compact": true, "cozy": true },
    "models": {
      "i18n": {
        "type": "sap.ui.model.resource.ResourceModel",
        "settings": { "bundleName": "app.list.i18n.i18n" }
      }
    },
    "routing": {
      "config": {
        "routerClass": "sap.f.routing.Router",
        "viewType": "XML",
        "viewPath": "app.list.view",
        "controlId": "layout",
        "controlAggregation": "beginColumnPages",
        "async": true,
        "bypassed": { "target": "list" }
      },
      "routes": [
        { "pattern": "", "name": "list", "target": "list" },
        { "pattern": "detail/{id}", "name": "detail", "target": "detail" }
      ],
      "targets": {
        "list": { "viewName": "List", "viewId": "list", "viewLevel": 1 },
        "detail": {
          "type": "Component",
          "usage": "detail",
          "controlAggregation": "midColumnPages"
        }
      }
    },
    "rootView": {
      "viewName": "app.list.view.App",
      "type": "XML",
      "async": true,
      "id": "app"
    }
  }
}

3. Feature Detail

Feature Detail also has an App view and an App controller, but in this example they do not contain any significant logic. Therefore, I omit them here. The same also applies to "Component.js".

Detail View

For this example, we would simply assume that the detail view is a simple ObjectPageLayout. Then, we embed additional modules in the individual sections, each within a ComponentContainer. For simplicity, I will only cover the Comments module.

The ComponentContainer automatically renders the Comments module which we specify in the usage property (“moduleComments” is the componentUsages name from the manifest).

<mvc:View
	controllerName="app.detail.controller.Detail"
	xmlns="sap.m"
	displayBlock="true"
	xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
	xmlns:uxap="sap.uxap"
	xmlns:core="sap.ui.core">
	<uxap:ObjectPageLayout
        id="objectPageLayout"
		showTitleInHeaderContent="true"		
		showFooter="true"
		alwaysShowContentHeader="true"
		preserveHeaderStateOnScroll="false"
		headerContentPinnable="true"
		isChildPage="true"
		upperCaseAnchorBar="false"
		useIconTabBar="true"
		enableLazyLoading="true">
		<uxap:headerTitle>
			<uxap:ObjectPageDynamicHeaderTitle
				id="objectPageDynamicHeaderTitle"
				areaShrinkRatio="5:1:1">
				<uxap:heading>
						<Title
							id="objectPageDynamicHeaderTitleHeadingTitle"
							wrapping="true"
							text="Feature Detail: {detailView>/id}"/>
				</uxap:heading>
			</uxap:ObjectPageDynamicHeaderTitle>
		</uxap:headerTitle>
		<uxap:headerContent>
		</uxap:headerContent>
		<uxap:sections>
		
			<!-- Modul Comments -->
			<uxap:ObjectPageSection title="Comments">
				<uxap:subSections>
					<uxap:ObjectPageSubSection mode="Expanded">
						<core:ComponentContainer
							id="moduleCommentsContainer"
							usage="moduleComments"
							async="true"
							componentCreated="onComponentCommentsCreated"/>
					</uxap:ObjectPageSubSection>
				</uxap:subSections>
			</uxap:ObjectPageSection>

			<!-- Modul History -->
			<uxap:ObjectPageSection title="History">
				<uxap:subSections>
					<uxap:ObjectPageSubSection mode="Expanded">
						<!-- <core:ComponentContainer
							id="moduleHistoryContainer"
							usage="moduleHistory"
							async="true"
							componentCreated="onComponentHistoryCreated"/> -->
							<Text text="History"/>
					</uxap:ObjectPageSubSection>
				</uxap:subSections>
			</uxap:ObjectPageSection>

			<!-- Modul Flow -->
			<uxap:ObjectPageSection title="Flow">
				<uxap:subSections>
					<uxap:ObjectPageSubSection mode="Expanded">
						<!-- <core:ComponentContainer
							id="moduleFlowContainer"
							usage="moduleFlow"
							async="true"
							componentCreated="onComponentFlowCreated"/> -->
							<Text text="Flow"/>
					</uxap:ObjectPageSubSection>
				</uxap:subSections>
			</uxap:ObjectPageSection>
		</uxap:sections>
		<uxap:footer>
			<OverflowToolbar>
			</OverflowToolbar>
		</uxap:footer>
	</uxap:ObjectPageLayout>
</mvc:View>

Detail Controller

There are multiple points here.

First, we implement a simple view binding (_bindView) where we bind the ID passed from List to our view.

Second, some functions for chapter 5 (communication) are already defined which trigger events in other components.
For example, setting or clearing the busy state in the Shell (_setShellBusy and _hideShellBusy) or controlling the layout in List (_listSetLayout).

And lastly, there are handler functions for the componentCreated event of the module ComponentContainer.

Here I am already anticipating something:

As soon as the Comments module was created, the function onComponentCommentsCreated is triggered. In this function, we pass an API created by "_createCommentsApi" to the module, through which "Comments" can call functions of the parent component.

This is one possible way of communication between components.

Another way, which I will discuss later, is accessing the required component via a defined ID. In our example we use a helper function called "getComponent". More on this later.

sap.ui.define([
	"app/utility/controller/BaseController",
	"sap/ui/model/json/JSONModel"
], function(
	BaseController,	
	JSONModel
) {
	"use strict";

	return BaseController.extend("app.detail.controller.Detail", {

		onInit: function() {
			const oViewModel = this._createViewModel();
			this.setModel(oViewModel, "detailView");

			this.getRouter().getRoute("detail").attachPatternMatched(this._handleRouteMatched, this);
		},	
		
		getEntityId: function() {
			return this.getModel("detailView").getProperty("/id");
		},

		_createViewModel: function() {
			const oViewModel = new JSONModel({
				busy: false,
				delay: 50,
				id: ""
			});
			return oViewModel;
		},

		_handleRouteMatched: async function(oEvent) {				
			try {
				this._listSetLayout("TwoColumnsMidExpanded");
				const sId = oEvent.getParameter("arguments").id;
				this._setShellBusy();
				await this._bindView(sId);
				await this._bindCommentsModule(sId);
			} catch(oError) {
				// Error handling
			} finally {
				this._hideShellBusy();
			}
		},

		_createCommentsApi: function () {
			return {
				getEntityId: this.getEntityId.bind(this),
				setShellBusy: this._setShellBusy.bind(this),
				hideShellBusy: this._hideShellBusy.bind(this)
			};
		},
		
		_bindView: async function(sId) {
			this.getModel("detailView").setProperty("/id", sId);
			return Promise.resolve(true);
		},
		
		_bindCommentsModule: async function(sId) {
			const oComponent = this.byId("moduleCommentsContainer").getComponentInstance();
			if(oComponent) {
				oComponent.fireSetEntityId({id: sId});									
			}
			return Promise.resolve(true);			
		},

		/* --- Shell Busy State Handling --- */
		_setShellBusy: function() {
			try {
				this.getComponent("app.shell").fireSetShellBusy();
			} catch (oError) {
				//error handling
			}
		},

		_hideShellBusy: function() {
			try {
				this.getComponent("app.shell").fireHideShellBusy();
			} catch (oError) {
				//error handling
			}
		},

		/* --- Feature List Events ---  */
		_listSetLayout: function(sLayout) {
			try {
				this.getComponent("app.list").fireSetLayout({layout: sLayout});
			} catch (oError) {
				//error handling
			}
		},

		/* --- Module Creation Handling --- */
		onComponentCommentsCreated: function(oEvent) {
			const oComponent = oEvent.getParameter("component");
			oComponent.oAPI = this._createCommentsApi();
			
			this._bindCommentsModule(this.getModel("detailView").getProperty("/id"));
		},		

		onComponentHistoryCreated: function(oEvent) {
		},

		onComponentFlowCreated: function() {
		}
	});
});

Detail Manifest

The Detail feature can contain additional modules that are used within this feature.

In our example, these would be Comments, History, and Flow, for which we have already created the appropriate ComponentContainers in the view. The modules are automatically loaded into the respective ComponentContainers when opening the Detail feature.

{
  "_version": "1.32.0",
  "sap.app": {
    "id": "app.detail",
    "type": "application",
    "i18n": "i18n/i18n.properties",
    "applicationVersion": {
      "version": "0.0.1"
    },
    "title": "{{appTitle}}",
    "description": "{{appDescription}}",
    "dataSources": {
      "mainService": {
        "uri": "/sap/opu/odata/SERVICE",
        "type": "OData",
        "settings": {
          "localUri": "localService/metadata.xml",
          "odataVersion": "2.0"
        }
      }
    }
  },
  "sap.ui": {
    "technology": "UI5",
    "fullWidth": true,
    "icons": {
      "icon": "",
      "favIcon": "",
      "phone": "",
      "phone@2": "",
      "tablet": "",
      "tablet@2": ""
    },
    "deviceTypes": {
      "desktop": true,
      "tablet": true,
      "phone": true
    }
  },
  "sap.ui5": {
    "flexEnabled": true,
    "dependencies": {
      "minUI5Version": "1.97.0",
      "libs": {
        "sap.ui.core": {},
        "sap.m": {},
        "sap.f": {},
        "sap.uxap": {}
      }
    },
    "componentUsages": {
      "moduleComments": {
        "name": "app.module.comments",
        "lazy": true
      },
      "moduleHistory": {
        "name": "app.module.history",
        "lazy": true
      },
      "moduleFlow": {
        "name": "app.module.flow",
        "lazy": true
      }
    },
    "contentDensities": {
      "compact": true,
      "cozy": true
    },
    "models": {
      "i18n": {
        "type": "sap.ui.model.resource.ResourceModel",
        "settings": {
          "bundleName": "app.detail.i18n.i18n"
        }
      }
    },
    "routing": {
      "config": {
        "routerClass": "sap.f.routing.Router",
        "viewType": "XML",
        "viewPath": "app.detail.view",
        "controlId": "app",
        "controlAggregation": "pages",        
        "async": true
      },
      "routes": [
        {
          "pattern": "Detail/{id}",
          "name": "detail",
          "target": [
            "detail"
          ]
        }
      ],
      "targets": {
        "detail": {
          "viewName": "Detail",
          "viewLevel": 1,
          "viewId": "detail"
        }
      }
    },
    "rootView": {
      "viewName": "app.detail.view.App",
      "type": "XML",
      "async": true,
      "id": "app"
    }
  }
}

4. Modules

Modules are smaller, reusable components that are used exclusively (at least in our example) within a feature.

What is defined as a module depends on the specific use case.

In our projects, modules are small functional components that are embedded in multiple features.

For example, a UI5 application with a table for change history, notes (in our example Comments), etc.

The module takes care of its own UI and data retrieval.

The structure can remain very clear and compact:

 

 

 

 

 

5. Communication between Components

This is the most important part.

For communication between components (e.g. passing data or triggering functions), there are multiple possibilities.

Some use the EventBus, others build an additional service class.

Here I want to explain two possibilities that we have used in our projects.

Event handling with sap.ui.core.ComponentRegistry

We define custom events in Component.js for functions that we want to use in the respective component.

The following scenario:

 

 

 

 

 

 

As soon as we click an entry in the list of Feature List, we want to see the details in Feature Detail on the right side.
For that, we need to set the layout of the FlexibleColumnLayout in the App.controller.js of Feature List to "TwoColumnsMidExpanded".

We already prepared the necessary steps.

1. Define the event "setLayout" in the List component.

metadata: {
  manifest: "json",
  events: {
	setLayout: {
	  parameters: {
		layout: {type: "string"}
	  }
	}
  }
}

2. Define the event handler in App.controller.js (List).

onInit: function () {
    //...
	this.getOwnerComponent().attachSetLayout(this._onSetLayout, this);
    //...
},

_onSetLayout: function (oEvent) {
	const sLayout = oEvent.getParameter("layout");
	this.getModel("appView").setProperty("/layout", sLayout);
},

To be able to use this event in Feature Detail, we created a helper function getComponent in our example.

getComponent: function (sAppId) {
  let oResultComponent;

  const aComponentNames = Object.keys(sap.ui.core.ComponentRegistry.all());
  aComponentNames.forEach((sComponentName) => {
    const oComponent = sap.ui.core.ComponentRegistry.all()[sComponentName];
    if (oComponent.getManifestEntry("sap.app").id === sAppId) {
      oResultComponent = oComponent;
    }
  });

  return oResultComponent;
}

We pass the app ID to this function, which we previously defined in the component’s manifest.json, and which identifies the component we want to access.

This function is reliable as long as app IDs are maintained properly and kept unique.

This helper can also be centralized. In our example, the function is located in the BaseController, which is outsourced into a separate Utility library.

Utility/
└─ src/
   └─ app/
      └─ utility/
         ├─ controller/
         │  └─ BaseController.js
         ├─ library.js
         ├─ manifest.json
         ├─ package.json
         └─ ui5.yaml

Since each feature inherits from the BaseController, Feature Detail can now trigger the layout event, for example.

_listSetLayout: function(sLayout) {
	try {
		this.getComponent("app.list").fireSetLayout({layout: sLayout});
	} catch (oError) {
		//error handling
	}
},

The ID "app.list" is the ID that we defined in the manifest.json of Feature List.

 "sap.app": {
    "id": "app.list"

As soon as the event is triggered, Feature Detail opens in the right area of the FlexibleColumnLayout.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Passing an API

Another possibility that we also like to use is passing an “API” to another component.

Here we create an object with callbacks and pass it to a newly created or rendered component.

We have already prepared this in Feature Detail as well:

_createCommentsApi: function () {
    return {
        getEntityId: this.getEntityId.bind(this),
        setShellBusy: this._setShellBusy.bind(this),
        hideShellBusy: this._hideShellBusy.bind(this)
    };
},

As soon as the Comments module was created by the ComponentContainer, we pass the API with the callbacks.

onComponentCommentsCreated: function(oEvent) {
    const oComponent = oEvent.getParameter("component");
    oComponent.oAPI = this._createCommentsApi();
     
    this._bindCommentsModule(this.getModel("detailView").getProperty("/id"));
},

This way, the Comments module is able to execute functions of the parent component.

A very rough example:

We set the busy state of the Shell before the view binding via the Feature Detail API in the function "_setEntityId" and clear it again afterwards.

Comments Controller:

sap.ui.define([
  "app/utility/controller/BaseController",
	"sap/ui/model/json/JSONModel"
], function (
    BaseController, 
    JSONModel
) {
    "use strict";

    return BaseController.extend("app.module.comments.controller.Comments", {

        onInit: function () {
            const oViewModel = this._createViewModel();
			this.setModel(oViewModel, "moduleView");

            this.getOwnerComponent().attachSetEntityId(this._setEntityId, this);
        },

        _setEntityId: function(oEvent) {
            this.getOwnerComponent().oAPI.setShellBusy();
            this.getModel("moduleView").setProperty("/id", oEvent.getParameter("id"));
            this.getOwnerComponent().oAPI.hideShellBusy();
        },

        _createViewModel: function() {
			const oViewModel = new JSONModel({
				busy: false,
				delay: 50,
				id: ""
			});
			return oViewModel;
		}
    });
});

6. Conclusion

The examples shown here are intended only as an inspiration, and I therefore kept them intentionally rough and simple.

If you notice early on (or already know) that the application will grow significantly in the future, it can be worth thinking about a larger architecture already at this stage.

If you wait too long, dependencies quickly arise and the restructuring becomes unnecessarily complex and time-consuming.

A clean structure does not only pay off in readability, but also makes later maintenance easier.

entry.title: SAP UX Q1/2026 Update – Part 2: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602 and SAP Fiori Launchpad
entry.content:
This second post in my series gives an overview of user experience innovations that become generally available with the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602 release. Highlights are further AI-assisted features: for personalizing My Home, getting explanations for error messages, and Situation Handling recommendations. We also provide the often-requested capability to launch apps via transaction code or SAP Fiori ID, the new “required reading” feature for news in My Home, and mobile phone optimization – to name a few.

Users in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition can use Joule to access their data: via web browser on desktop, or mobile, via SAP Mobile Start, which also provides access to applications directly. Read about innovations for Joule and SAP Mobile Start in the first blog post introducing the series, and providing information about SAP Build Work Zone:

  • SAP UX Q1/2026 Update – Part 1: AI, Joule, SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Mobile Start.

Below, you will get an overview of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition UX innovations in these areas:

SAP Fiori launchpad
  o.  New shell bar now the default
  o.  Launch apps directly via Transaction Code / Fiori-ID
  o.  Making New Apps Available to Users
      -  Activating new apps, since now delivered inactive
      -  New Experience Switch
   o.  Further improvements for the launchpad
My Home
  o.  Manage News app now with “must be read” feature
  o.  Enhanced Personalization
  o.  AI-Assisted UX Features:
       -  AI-Assisted Smart Personalization of My Home for Applications
       -  AI-Assisted Error Explanation
       -  AI-Assisted Situation Handling
SAP Fiori elements mobile phone optimization
UI Adaptation
Improvements in details

Beyond these, we have a number of interesting new UX innovations for beta testing and early adopters with the 2602 release. I plan to cover these in my next blog post in the next few days.

Many of the UX improvements in details are enabled by our UI framework (SAPUI5 and SAP Fiori elements) and hence also available to customers and partners developing their own applications. Note however that the enabled AI-assisted features are only available for extensions built directly on the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition system.

Below you’ll see AI innovations from our UX framework as well as some selected application highlights. You can get a more comprehensive overview of the latest AI innovations in these blog posts, which also include application specific examples:

  • Highlights of the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602 release.
  • AI innovations highlights in SAP Cloud ERP 2602.
  • Artificial Intelligence in SAP Cloud ERP 2602.

 

SAP Fiori Launchpad

Let’s look closer at these UX updates:

  • New shell bar now the default, with updated design of notifications and user menu.
  • Launch apps directly via Transaction Code / Fiori-ID.
  • Further improvements.

New shell bar now the default

We have made design improvements which are relevant for a consistent user experience across the entire SAP Business Suite. The new shell bar first became available as an opt-in choice for customers with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2508 as described in my blog post covering 2508. With the 2602 release, it is now activated by default.

Benefits of the New Shell Bar

The new shell bar has these benefits:

  • The product name is displayed prominently now to the right of the SAP logo. In this case the name is “S/4HANA Cloud”.
  • Updated notifications design.
  • Updated user menu design.

Product Name Displayed

The immediately visible difference with the new shell bar is that the product name is displayed to the right of the SAP Logo, in this case “S/4HANA Cloud” as you can see at the top of Figure 1. The logo and the name are all contained within a clickable area, which brings the user to the start page (i.e. My Home).

Figure 1: New shell bar, now activated by default. Alt Text: A screenshot of Manage Sales Orders app, with the shell header highlighted, looking as described in the text.

Updated User Menu

The new user menu design shown in Figure 2 provides more space for the avatar of the user, and provides more space between items for easier selection. The settings are always at the top as the first entry, and the “Sign Out” option is separated from the actual navigation options in the list, to make it easier to find.

 

Figure 2: Updated design of user menu and notifications. Alt Text: On the left an image of the previous user menu and the updated user menu. On the right an image of the updated notifications list, showing four notifications in the group “Last Month”, each with a title, two lines of description and a line with the data and a “more” link.

Updated Notifications Design

The new simplified design of notifications shown on the right in Figure 2  fits better with the overall design of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, and provides simplier interactions. By default notifications are sorted by date, with grouping by “Today”, “Yesterday”, “Last Week”, “Last Month”, as you can see in Figure 3 below. Users can also choose to sort by importance.

 Figure 3: Updated design of notifications. Alt Text: A short video showing the user selecting to display the list of notifications in the groups “Today”, “Yesterday” and “This Week”. 

Recommendation: Check Test Automation and RPA

We advise that you check any test automation and RPA (Robotic Process Automation) you may have, since you may need to make some adjustments – in particular if you access the user menu, for example to log off at the end of a test.

In case you need more time to do this, you can opt-out of the new shell bar for this release.You can switch it off via “Activate New Features”, Feature “/UI2/SHELL_BAR_TOGGLE”.

Important: do switch it back on before next upgrade! There is no opt-out planned from 2608 onwards.

Launch apps directly via Transaction Code

Customers have been requesting this for quite a while now, so I am happy to announce that with the 2602 release, users can launch applications directly via the transaction code (t-code).

This is particularly helpful for those users who have experience with older SAP ERP systems, such as SAP ECC (ERP Central Component), and are used to launching transactions by entering the “t-code” (transaction code) in the SAP GUI for Windows shell bar. To launch a transaction VA01 (Create sales order) in SAP GUI for Windows, users enter “/nVA01” if they want it to open in the same window, and “/oVA01” if they want it to open in a new window. Lower case “/nva01” or “/ova01” also works. Furthermore, users could go to the start page by entering “/n” or “/o”, opening it either in the same window or in a new window respectively.

We have implemented the same logic in the search field in the launchpad shell bar, but with “/o” now creating a new browser tab rather than opening a new window.

We have also extended it, so that users can also enter the t-code after “/n” or “/o”. Figure 4 shows what it looks like, and Figure 5 shows a short video of it in action for an example using /nmm03 (Display Material) to open the classic UI and /nF217A (Customer – 360 View) to open a Fiori app.

 

Figure 4: Launching a classic UI via the transaction code “va01”. Alt Text: The shell header is shown, with the search field showing “All” and the search term /nva01 entered.

 

Figure 5: Launching applications directly via transaction code. Alt Text: A short video showing the user entering /nmm03 and /nF217A as described in the text.

You can determine an application’s t-code via the About entry in the launchpad user menu. We have also assigned transaction codes to Fiori apps, they are often identical to the Fiori ID, but not always.

Read more about it in this blog post:

  • Navigate SAP Fiori launchpad with Ease: Launch Apps via Transaction Codes.

Making New Apps Available to Users

New apps for business users can help them work more effectively, and get their work done more easily – but only if they actually use them! That means that they need to be activated and hence made available to users in the first place.

When one or more new applications are delivered that replace an existing application, users of the existing application need to be informed that a replacement is available to them. This is handled by the New Experience Switch. This is especially important if the existing application is about to be deprecated.

The next two sections cover these two topics in more detail: activating new apps, and the New Experience Switch.

Activating new apps, since now delivered inactive

With the 2602 release we no longer activate new apps automatically, so as not to disrupt existing users, as well as to ensure that users are not automatically granted authorizations that they should not have.

In order to activate an application, i.e. a Fiori app or a classic UI, the underlying IAM  (Identity and Application Management) apps of type “App Authorization Variant” need to be activated. These particular IAM apps were introduced a while ago in order to allow finer-grained authorizations for applications.

For example, the My Inbox app has a standard view and an expert view. You will only be able to access the expert view if the corresponding IAM app for the expert view has been activated by your administrator. If your administrator has only activated the IAM app for the standard view, then you will be able to access the My Inbox app, but only its standard view.

Since new apps provided by SAP are no longer activated automatically, administrators need to activate the underlying IAM apps of type “App Authorization Variant”. Only then will users be able to find the apps via the launchpad search or in the App Finder, see the corresponding tiles on pages in the launchpad, and assign them as favorites in My Home.

Find out more in the documentation:

  • Work with IAM Apps (App Authorization Variants)
  • How to Activate or Deactivate IAM Apps (App Authorization Variants)

New Experience Switch

The New Experience Switch informs users that a replacement of the application that they have opened is available to them, and offers a toggle in the shell bar to directly switch to the new application. You can see what this looks like in the top left image in Figure 6.

If the existing, old version of the app is planned to be deprecated and removed in a future release beyond the next one, then the information icon in the shell bar next to the New Version toggle is replaced by a warning icon, as shown in the top right image in Figure 6.

Finally, if the existing, old version will be removed with the upgrade to the next release, an alert is shown instead of a warning, as shown in the bottom image in Figure 6.

Figure 6: New Experience Switch. Clockwise from top left: Information, Warning and Alert pop-overs. Alt Text: Three screenshots showing the respective popovers, each with a button “Switch to New Version”. The first two include a carousel giving information about the benefits of the new version. The last one include in red “August 2025” as the month when the app is planned to be removed.

The three phases described above were introduced already with the 2508 release.

With the 2602 release, we also support cases where one application has multiple successors. Figure 7 shows an example.

Figure 7: New Experience Switch warning for an application with multiple successors. Alt Text: Screenshot showing the popover containing two cards, one for each of the specialized versions available as successors.

Also new with 2602 is the option for users to provide feedback when the open up the new version of an application (Figure 8). When they select this, a survey is opened with one page of questions about the new version of the app.

Figure 8: New Experience Switch welcome pop-over for new app, now with “Provide Feedback” button. Alt Text: Screenshot showing the pop-over, with a carousel giving information about the benefits of the new version.
